17: A direct revelation: I have not come to abolish the Law and the Prophets but to fulfill them. Christ's relationship to Scripture is unique: he is not a scribe who interprets it, not a prophet who applies it, but the one toward whom all of it was pointing and in whom all of it reaches its intended fullness. The antitheses that follow (You have heard it said... but I say to you) do not set Jesus against Moses but demonstrate Jesus's sovereign authority to reveal the Torah's deepest intention — the intention the Author always had, which only the Author himself can fully declare.
48: A direct revelation: be perfect as your heavenly Father is perfect. The standard of the kingdom's ethics is not a moral code but a person — the character of the Father, which is also the character of the Son. Jesus does not merely command an impossible ethical ideal; he embodies it. The Sermon on the Mount describes not only what his disciples must become but what he already is: poor in spirit, meek, merciful, pure in heart, a peacemaker who endures persecution for righteousness. He is both the teacher and the subject of every beatitude.
1: A type: Jesus ascending the mountain to deliver the kingdom-Torah mirrors Moses ascending Sinai to receive the Law (Exodus 19:3; Matthew 24:12). Matthew has been constructing this New Moses typology from the flight to Egypt and back (Matthew 2:14–15), the crossing of water at baptism, the forty-day testing in the wilderness. The mountain-seat is the throne of the authoritative teacher; but unlike Moses, Jesus will speak on his own authority.
2: A revelation of God: "he opened his mouth and taught them" echoes the formal opening of authoritative divine speech. The content that follows is not Torah transmitted but the announcement of what the Law always aimed at — the life of the kingdom as Christ embodies it. The Teacher here is also the subject of the teaching.
3: A fulfillment: Luke 4:18–19 records Jesus citing Isaiah 61:1–2 ("he has anointed me to preach good news to the poor") as fulfilled in himself. The first beatitude draws on the same Isaianic tradition of the anawim — the poor, humble ones who receive God's favor. The kingdom of heaven belongs to those who know they cannot bring it about; Christ is the one who brings it to them.
4: A fulfillment: Isaiah 61:2–3 promised that the anointed one would "comfort all who mourn" and give them "a garland instead of ashes." The comfort is eschatological — it arrives with the Messiah. This beatitude is not advice for managing grief but an announcement that the one who comforts has come.
5: A fulfillment: Psalms 37:11 ("the meek shall inherit the land") is a promise to the faithful remnant who trust God rather than seize by force. The NT reading is that the land-promise has been expanded to the whole renewed creation (Romans 4:13), and the meek who receive it are those who enter the kingdom Christ opens. The inheritance comes through him, not through them.
6: A fulfillment: Isaiah 55:1–2 invites the thirsty and hungry to come and eat what only God can give — covenant satisfaction that money cannot buy. The beatitude draws on that Isaianic promise and announces its arrival. Christ is both the one who pronounces the blessing and, as the righteous one, the ground of the righteousness his people receive (2 Corinthians 5:21).
7: The theme of hesed — covenant mercy — runs from Exodus 34:6–7 through the Psalms and Prophets as the defining attribute of God toward his covenant people. The beatitude promises that those who embody this mercy will receive it. Christ is the definitive demonstration of divine mercy (Romans 9:15–16) and the one through whom his people become merciful.
8: A fulfillment: Psalms 24:3–4 asks who may ascend the hill of the LORD — "he who has clean hands and a pure heart." The beatitude echoes this entrance criterion and announces its fulfillment: the pure in heart will see God. This eschatological vision is made possible through Christ who purifies the heart (Acts 15:9; Hebrews 10:22) and is himself the image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15).
9: A revelation of God: peacemakers are called sons of God because they reflect the character of the God who reconciles. The NT identifies Christ as the eirēnopoiēsas, the one who makes peace through the blood of his cross (Colossians 1:20). Those who make peace share in the family vocation of God's firstborn Son (Romans 8:29).
10: A theme: the prophets were persecuted for righteousness, and so is the Servant of Isaiah (Isaiah 50:6; Matthew 53:3). The kingdom of heaven belongs to the persecuted because they belong to the suffering Servant who has already entered the kingdom through suffering. Persecution for righteousness is participation in the pattern Christ established (1 Peter 2:21).
11: A direct claim: the shift to second person ("blessed are you") and the explicit "because of me" makes this a Christological criterion. Persecution that qualifies for the beatitude is specifically suffering on account of Jesus — not suffering in general, but suffering caused by alignment with him. The blessedness is constituted by relationship to his person.
12: A theme: the long tradition of prophetic suffering (1 Kings 18:4; Nehemiah 9:26; Jer 20) is the lineage the disciples enter. Christ stands at the end of that line as the prophet par excellence who was rejected and killed (Matthew 21:37–39). Rejoicing in persecution is possible because the pattern ends not in death but in vindication — as Christ's own resurrection demonstrates.
13: A theme: Israel was called to be a covenant community seasoning the world with divine righteousness (cf. the salt of the covenant, Numbers 18:19; Leviticus 2:13). The disciple community now carries this vocation through Christ, who himself is the covenant salt (Mark 9:49–50). Saltiness that cannot be restored mirrors the covenant that cannot be renewed by human effort — it requires the renewing work of Christ.
14: A revelation of God: Isaiah's Servant is appointed "a light to the nations" (Isaiah 42:6; Matthew 49:6), and Jesus applies this Servant vocation to his disciple community. But John's Gospel identifies Jesus himself as the light of the world (John 8:12; Matthew 9:5). The disciples are derivative light — they illuminate because he does. A city on a hill cannot be hidden; neither can the one who brought it into being.
15: A theme: the lamp-on-stand image is proverbial wisdom, but its Christological weight lies in what the lamp reveals. The light that must not be hidden is ultimately the light of the knowledge of the glory of God displayed in the face of Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6). The community bears this light; Christ is its source.
16: A revelation of God: the goal of visible good works is the glorification of the Father, not self-commendation. This is the pattern Jesus models throughout his ministry — everything he does points to the Father (John 5:19; Matthew 8:29). The disciple community, formed by Christ, continues his doxological mission.
18: A revelation of God: not one iota or serif will pass from the Law "until all is accomplished" (heōs an panta genētai) — binding the Law's fulfillment to a historical completion event. The cross is where "it is finished" (tetelestai, John 19:30) and where the Law's demands are fully met (Colossians 2:14). The permanence of Torah is guaranteed by the one who fulfills it completely.
19: A theme: the kingdom of heaven operates under the commands Christ delivers and interprets. Those who set aside the least of these commands misunderstand who gave them and who fulfills them. Greatness in this kingdom is calibrated by faithfulness to the one who embodies all righteousness.
20: A direct claim: the righteousness that exceeds the scribes and Pharisees is not quantitatively more rule-keeping but a different kind of righteousness altogether — the gift of right standing that comes through Christ (Philippians 3:9; 2 Corinthians 5:21). Jesus announces the standard of the kingdom he is establishing, a standard only he can meet and only he can impart.
21: A revelation of God: Jesus quotes the sixth commandment (Exodus 20:13) and extends it on his own authority — "But I say to you." This is not the prophetic formula (thus says the LORD) but the voice of the one who gave the Law. The antitheses do not set Torah aside; they reveal what it always required from the one who alone can see the heart.
22: A revelation of God: inward anger and contemptuous speech are subject to the same judgment as murder because Christ reveals the interior dimension of the commandment. The one who says "You fool!" faces Gehenna — language that only makes sense from the lips of the judge who knows the heart. Jesus speaks as the one who will adjudicate these very matters (Matthew 25:31–46).
23: A theme: reconciliation placed above cultic worship is a consistent prophetic priority (Isaiah 1:13–17; Hosea 6:6). Christ, who is both priest and sacrifice in the final offering (Hebrews 10:10), is also the one who provides the ultimate basis for human reconciliation (Ephesians 2:14–16). The altar-gift instruction points toward the offering that makes all reconciliation possible.
24: A theme: reconciliation precedes offering — the sequence that the sacrificial system aimed toward but could not achieve. The pattern points forward to the one who would be both the reconciling act and the offering: Christ's death is simultaneously the means of reconciliation with God and the foundation of human reconciliation (2 Corinthians 5:18–19).
25: A theme: the urgency of settling with one's adversary before reaching the judge reflects the eschatological urgency that runs through the Sermon — the kingdom is present, the Judge is already active. This urgency is anchored in who Jesus is: the coming Judge whose arrival makes the moment decisive.
26: A theme: the prison from which there is no exit until the last penny is paid is eschatological consequence-language — the seriousness of unresolved sin before the judgment Christ will execute. The way of escape is the way of reconciliation, the very thing Christ provides through his atoning work (Matthew 26:28).
27: A revelation of God: the seventh commandment (Exodus 20:14) is quoted as the baseline from which Jesus will move to the interior. As with Matthew 5:21, the voice that extends the commandment is the same voice that gave it. The sequence — you have heard, but I say — identifies Jesus with the divine legislator who alone can authorize such an extension.
28: A revelation of God: lustful looking is adultery in the heart because Christ, who gave the command, sees the heart (1 Samuel 16:7; Jeremiah 17:10). This is not a new external rule but a revelation of what the commandment always addressed — the will oriented toward another person as an object. The standard is the standard of God's own purity, which only Christ fully embodies.
29: A theme: the hyperbolic language ("gouge out," "cut off") is not literal surgery but radical prioritization of the kingdom over bodily comfort. Christ embodies the ultimate sacrifice of body for the sake of others — the cross where his own body is given so that his people are not thrown into Gehenna. The logic of self-sacrifice he teaches here he enacts completely.
30: A theme: no part of the body is worth preserving at the cost of losing everything. This is the economics of the kingdom that Christ teaches and embodies: lose your life to save it (Matthew 16:25). The one who calls his disciples to this radical calculus has himself paid the full cost.
31: A theme: Jesus engages the Mosaic divorce regulation (Deuteronomy 24:1–4) not to abolish it but to restrict it to its intended scope, restoring the creation order of Gen 1–2 that divorce disrupts (Matthew 19:4–6). Christ as the new Adam restores what the old Adam lost — including the integrity of the covenant bond that marriage images.
32: A revelation of God: the exception clause and the adultery-consequence reveal Christ's understanding of marriage as a covenant bond (Malachi 2:14–16) that cannot be dissolved without covenantal rupture. This mirrors the faithfulness of God to his covenant people — the divorce God declares in Jeremiah 3:8 is the extreme end of covenant violation, not the norm. Christ upholds the covenant logic of permanence.
33: A revelation of God: the oath-system built on Leviticus 19:12 and Numbers 30:2 invoked divine witness to guarantee human speech. Jesus will now replace the entire apparatus with the truthfulness that characterizes life in God's kingdom — which Christ himself perfectly embodies ("I am the truth," John 14:6).
34: A revelation of God: "do not swear at all" removes the distinction between sacred and secular speech — every word is already spoken before God. Christ teaches an integrated integrity that he models completely. No oath can add to the truth of what God's own Son speaks.
35: A theme: the earth as God's footstool and Jerusalem as the city of the Great King (Psalms 48:2) frame the prohibition in God's universal sovereignty. That sovereignty is not invoked by oath formulas but by life-orientation toward the King. The kingdom Jesus announces is the in-breaking of this reign — and Jesus is the one in whom the King arrives (Matthew 21:5).
36: A theme: the inability to make a single hair white or black reveals human finitude before the divine absolute. Jesus grounds the prohibition of self-invoking oaths in the reality that humans have nothing to swear by. The one who speaks here is the one through whom every hair is numbered (Matthew 10:30) — the difference between creature and Creator which the oath-system was meant to honor.
37: A revelation of God: "let your yes be yes and your no be no" is the speech of one who needs no external guarantor because their words are self-authenticating. Jesus speaks this way throughout the Sermon — not "God has said" but "I say to you." His own yes is absolute (2 Corinthians 1:19–20: "all the promises of God are Yes in Christ").
38: A revelation of God: the lex talionis (Exodus 21:24; Leviticus 24:20; Deuteronomy 19:21) originally capped disproportionate retaliation. Jesus now replaces even the capped retaliation with non-resistance — absorbing evil rather than matching it. This is the movement of the cross, where God in Christ bears the full weight of human violence without retaliation (1 Peter 2:23).
39: A revelation of God: "do not resist an evil person" is the ethics of the one who will not resist arrest, beating, or crucifixion (Matthew 26:52–54). The right cheek turned is the right cheek Jesus turns in the passion. What he commands his disciples to embody, he enacts in the most extreme form. Non-retaliation is not passivity but the power of the God who overcomes evil with good (Romans 12:21).
40: A theme: giving the coat as well as the shirt inverts the logic of acquisition. The disciple community embodies the generosity by which Christ "though he was rich, became poor" so that others might be enriched (2 Corinthians 8:9). The ethics of kingdom generosity are grounded in what Christ himself does.
41: A theme: the compelled second mile converts legal obligation into an opportunity for grace — seizing occasion for generosity within or beyond compulsion. Christ goes the ultimate second mile by giving not just his labor but his life; this is the spirit that forms his community.
42: A revelation of God: "give to the one who asks you" reflects the character of the Father who gives to all who ask (Matthew 7:11), and who gives without restraint in the gift of his Son (John 3:16). The disciple who gives freely participates in the generosity of God that is fully expressed in Christ.
43: A revelation of God: the command "love your neighbor" (Leviticus 19:18) had been narrowed in popular interpretation to the in-group. Jesus corrects both the misreading and the underlying logic. The one who delivers this teaching will define his enemies as those for whom he prays and dies (Luke 23:34; Romans 5:10).
44: A direct claim: "love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you" is the ethics that Christ embodies completely at the cross. He does not merely teach enemy-love as an abstract principle; he enacts it in the most extreme case (Luke 23:34; Romans 5:8). The command is not separable from the one who gives it and models it.
45: A revelation of God: the Father sends sun and rain on the evil and the good — providential indiscriminate care reflecting divine generosity unconditioned by desert. This is the character of God that Christ reveals (John 1:14, 18) and that his people are called to reflect. The Father's impartial provision is the ground of enemy-love, not merely its analogy.
46: A theme: the tax collectors are used as the baseline of reciprocal love — even the marginalized can love those who love them back. Christ befriends tax collectors (Matthew 9:10–11) not because they love him first but because he loves them first (1 John 4:19), modeling the beyond-reciprocal love he demands.
47: A theme: even Gentiles greet their own. The disciple community is to embody a quality of relationship that crosses ethnic, social, and religious boundaries — which is precisely what Christ's mission accomplishes (Matthew 28:19; Galatians 3:28). The Sermon already anticipates the Gentile inclusion that Jesus brings.
